segments.title: How four G3 hulls might become aircraft carriers
segments.summary: A speculative G3 conversion shows how altered treaty tonnage could reshape British carrier strength and provoke matching American and Japanese responses.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses a fan-drawn G3 carrier to explore what four conversions might have meant for the Royal Navy. The scenario rests on his proposed 10:10:7 ratio and a 33,000-ton carrier ceiling, rather than the historical treaty settlement. The drawing uses separate forward command and aft funnel islands, although he suspects a real British conversion would have adopted a single island farther aft. He also stresses that a large British conversion program would affect American and Japanese choices, making carrier policy a competitive system rather than a national decision in isolation.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses four G3 carrier conversions, the proposed tonnage ratio, and the twin-island drawing.

segments.title: Why the 35,000-ton battleship limit looked unrealistic
segments.summary: The historical 35,000-ton ceiling is criticized for forcing every navy below the displacement its own requirements appeared to demand.
segments.body: The argument is not simply that treaty ships required compromise, but that contemporary design work was already clustering around roughly 40,000 to 43,000 tons. Dr. Clarke argues that knowingly imposing an inadequate ceiling risks shifting the cost of idealistic arms limitation onto sailors in a future war. Making every navy accept a poor design does not make the danger morally equal because each government remains responsible for its own personnel. His alternative begins from the premise that a peace-minded treaty must still permit viable warships if complete disarmament is unattainable.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts the 35,000-ton limit with larger design requirements and argues for realistic preparation.

segments.title: Japan's security case for a 7:10 fleet ratio
segments.summary: Japan's requested ratio is presented as enough strength to make an American attack uncertain, not enough to defeat superior industrial power in a long war.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts the historical 5:5:3 framework with Japan's desire for seven-tenths of American strength. In his reading, Japanese leaders feared isolation after the end of the Anglo-Japanese Alliance and wanted a fleet capable of making an opening attack prohibitively risky. The ratio was not expected to overcome British or American industrial depth in a long war, but to deny either power confidence in a quick first-round victory. He also argues that Japan's relatively successful First World War experience differed sharply from Europe's trauma, helping explain why the same treaty bargain could be perceived differently in Tokyo.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Japanese fear of attack, the alliance's end, and Japan's different wartime experience.

segments.title: Why an arms-control treaty cannot maximize one side's victory
segments.summary: A durable naval treaty must leave every signatory with enough advantage to prefer compliance over withdrawal or rearmament.
segments.body: American intelligence helped negotiators identify how far other powers could be pushed, but Dr. Clarke warns that winning the bargain too decisively can destroy its durability. States comply while the arrangement remains in their interest. A signatory repeatedly held to its bare minimum eventually has little incentive to preserve the agreement. A negotiator may therefore need to surrender part of a possible national advantage to reduce the chance that a dissatisfied signatory later abandons the system.

evidence.note: The transcript links treaty durability to every nation's incentive to remain within it.

segments.title: A 42,000-ton capital-ship settlement with staged replacement
segments.summary: The proposed settlement raises the battleship ceiling to 42,000 tons, adopts a 20:20:14:8:8 distribution, and phases replacement by ship age.
segments.body: HMS Hood's reported displacement supplies the precedent for rounding the capital-ship ceiling to 42,000 tons while retaining 16-inch guns. Scaling Japan upward leads Dr. Clarke to propose twenty ships each for Britain and the United States, fourteen for Japan, and eight each for France and Italy. The replacement rules apply the phased precedent offered to France and Italy to every signatory and prevent any navy from building more than one-fifth of its total allowance at once. A staged schedule would retire the least viable pre-dreadnought and wartime tonnage first, while an eighteen-year service life eventually sustains a regular construction rhythm.

evidence.note: The transcript states the 42,000-ton ceiling, revised ratios, and phased replacement dates.

segments.title: Why undersized capital ships would become death traps
segments.summary: A 42,000-ton, 16-inch-gun standard would make surviving 20,000-ton ships with 12- or 13-inch guns dangerously ineffective.
segments.body: The revised ceiling changes the value of older and smaller capital ships, not merely the specifications of future construction. Dr. Clarke argues that roughly 20,000-ton vessels armed with 12- or 13-inch guns could not fight effectively in a world of 42,000-ton ships carrying 16-inch guns. He describes them as little better than death traps rather than useful treaty tonnage. That mismatch would shape British, Japanese, and American calculations about which ships to retain and which to replace first.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts 20,000-ton ships with 12- or 13-inch guns against the proposed 42,000-ton, 16-inch standard.

segments.title: Why a steady shipbuilding drumbeat preserves naval capacity
segments.summary: Regular replacement spreads the cost of skilled labor, infrastructure, research, and surge capacity across more hulls than stop-start procurement.
segments.body: The treaty design is tied directly to industrial policy: Britain and the United States need enough recurring construction to retain their ability to build major warships. Dr. Clarke uses the Type 26 programs in Britain, Canada, and Australia as a modern analogy for deliberately slow production that keeps yards and skills alive. Stop-start procurement forces a navy to pay repeatedly to rebuild industrial capability whenever a new class is required. Individual ships can look expensive because the project carries the cost of maintaining complex infrastructure, research, experience, and future surge capacity, while a larger continuous program distributes those costs more widely.

evidence.note: The transcript connects replacement timing, Type 26 production, industrial continuity, and per-hull costs.

segments.title: G3 conversions and an alternative Royal Navy fast fleet
segments.summary: Alternative G3 completion and carrier-conversion combinations produce a striking interwar fast fleet alongside Hood, Tiger, Renown, and Repulse.
segments.body: One illustrated scenario completes a G3 as a surface warship while converting three sister hulls into carriers. Dr. Clarke imagines how those ships might operate and even appear ceremonially with Hood, Tiger, Renown, and Repulse. The imagined lineup places a G3 opposite Renown, Hood opposite Repulse, and the Jutland veteran Tiger between them. The watch point makes the treaty's abstract tonnage choices tangible by showing the fleet composition that different disposal and conversion rules could permit.

evidence.note: The transcript describes three G3 carriers, one completed G3, and the accompanying fast fleet.

segments.title: Carrier tonnage, conversions, and the transition to new builds
segments.summary: The historical carrier rules are contrasted with a 10:10:7:4:4 allocation and a staged shift from converted hulls to purpose-built carriers.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that treaty delegates were unusually restrictive toward carriers even though existing projects did not approach the permitted maximum. Combining battleships and battlecruisers within one capital-ship category also disadvantaged navies that already used both types and encouraged the later fast battleship. The alternative allocation translates the same 10:10:7:4:4 ratio into carrier tonnage while limiting construction in progress to one-fifth of a navy's allowance. It applies a 33,000-ton unit ceiling, requires early carriers to be conversions, permits a mixed program after 1926, and allows wholly new construction after 1930.

evidence.note: The transcript critiques historical carrier limits and sets out the alternative allocation and construction phases.

segments.title: A carrier-conversion loophole and the treaty's enforcement gap
segments.summary: The treaty's use of permissive conversion language suggests a possible new-build loophole whose practical restraint depended on political pressure rather than automatic enforcement.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke pauses over the wording that contracting powers may use two otherwise-scrapped ships for 33,000-ton carrier conversions. Because the clause says they may use those hulls rather than must use them, he sees a possible reading that permits two large carriers built from scratch. He expects other signatories would challenge any attempt to exploit that interpretation. The episode exposes a wider weakness: in 1922 the treaty ultimately relied on the threat of war even though few governments were likely to fight over an ambiguous carrier clause.

evidence.note: The transcript examines the word may in the carrier clause, a possible new-build reading, and the weakness of enforcement by threat of war.

segments.title: Six British conversions and a six-Lexington carrier force
segments.summary: Higher treaty allowances open paths to six British battlecruiser conversions or all six Lexington-class hulls entering service as American carriers.
segments.body: The British calculation combines three Admiral-class conversions with Furious, Courageous, and Glorious, consuming 171,000 tons of a 330,000-ton allowance while leaving room for Argus, Eagle, Hermes, or other carriers. The American counterpart converts every Lexington-class battlecruiser, producing Lexington, Constellation, Saratoga, Ranger, Constitution, and United States as an initial carrier force. Six conversions would give the United States a powerful foundation while still leaving room to build toward a ten-carrier allowance. These alternatives illustrate how generous conversion rules could accelerate naval aviation well before the historical carrier race.

evidence.note: The transcript calculates British conversion tonnage and names a six-ship Lexington conversion fleet.

segments.title: Regional carrier ratios depend on fleet concentration
segments.summary: Japan could concentrate seven carriers in the western Pacific, but combined or reinforced Anglo-American forces could erase that local numerical advantage.
segments.body: The 10:10:7 allocation gives Japan a plausible local carrier advantage without creating global superiority. Dr. Clarke imagines seven Japanese carriers concentrated in the western Pacific against perhaps five American carriers in the eastern Pacific and three British carriers in the Indian Ocean. If the British and American detachments combine, the balance becomes eight to seven before further reinforcement. The example shows why theater distribution and coalition concentration matter as much as the treaty's headline fleet ratio.

evidence.note: The transcript compares Japanese, American, and British carrier concentrations across the Pacific and Indian Ocean.

segments.title: Shipbuilding as economic support and strategic resilience
segments.summary: Continued warship construction is presented as a way to advance technology, sustain employment, and leave treaty powers better prepared for 1930s rearmament.
segments.body: A less restrictive treaty would keep money, technical experience, and productive infrastructure moving through national economies. Dr. Clarke suggests this continuity could soften economic shocks and increase the industrial gap that later fascist rearmament would have to overcome. Continued building also helps treaty powers feel capable of meeting their own requirements while common limits prevent any one fleet from becoming overwhelmingly strong. Cruisers remain limited by size and gun caliber rather than total numbers, so sustained cruiser orders could also compensate partly for reduced battleship and carrier work when governments supply the funding.

evidence.note: The transcript links construction with technology, employment, infrastructure, deterrence, and cruiser output.

segments.title: Pacific fortification limits and Japanese insecurity
segments.summary: Restrictions on Japan's former German islands compounded its inferior fleet ratio by making recently won territories feel exposed to reversal.
segments.body: Britain and the United States retained stronger regional bases, while Japan was restricted from fortifying islands gained from Germany during the First World War. Even if many island positions could be bypassed operationally, Dr. Clarke argues that their vulnerability mattered politically and emotionally. The settlement effectively made Japan's wartime gains feel revocable because another power could take them while Japan lacked the means to impose a serious cost. His revised clause permits modernization within the existing base area, full disclosure, and defensive weapons up to eight-inch cruiser caliber, giving an attacker a meaningful obstacle without enabling unlimited expansion.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Japan's fortification problem and reads the proposed modernization and defense clause.

segments.title: Why national emotion matters in treaty design
segments.summary: Treaty outcomes depend on collective fear, confidence, and humiliation as well as apparently rational calculations of tonnage and force ratios.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke defends treating states as communities of people whose policy is shaped by culture, worldview, and emotion. He compares this to managing a classroom's shared mood and invokes the emotional legacy of Versailles as a warning that formally logical settlements can produce destabilizing resentment. Weimar Germany remained capable of a different future, but accumulated grievance helped make it fertile ground for Nazism. With the United States outside the League of Nations, he argues that Britain, France, Italy, and Japan needed enough confidence and strength to support the interwar security order rather than feel bound or diminished by it.

evidence.note: The transcript connects collective emotion, Versailles, the League of Nations, and perceptions of naval strength.

segments.title: Balanced deterrence as the equitable treaty's goal
segments.summary: The final design gives each power enough viable ships and construction freedom to feel secure, but not enough superiority to attack confidently.
segments.body: The proposed system aims for a narrow political balance: each signatory should believe it gained enough to remain invested in the treaty, without gaining so much that it can exploit the others. Larger viable capital ships and revised ratios supply confidence, while continuing construction supports employment, yards, and naval readiness. British or American construction could proceed slowly enough to remain affordable, and American congressional reluctance would probably prevent a sustained Anglo-American naval race. Dr. Clarke tests the balance across the Mediterranean, Atlantic, Indian Ocean, and Pacific, concluding that every power could defend important interests but none could concentrate without exposing another theater.

evidence.note: The transcript summarizes the balance principle and tests force distributions across several theaters.

segments.title: How to build a historically bounded treaty counterfactual
segments.summary: The alternative treaty must grow from positions and precedents visible at the Washington conference rather than from an unconstrained ideal settlement.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke closes by defining the rules of his counterfactual. Suggestions must fit the conference's political context, the proposals actually discussed, and the decisions negotiators could plausibly have made. Judging the delegates against a settlement invented without those constraints would be historically unfair. The exercise is therefore a moderate revision using the participants' own logic, not a present-day wish list imposed on the past.

evidence.note: The transcript states that alternatives must remain grounded in the conference's actual context and ideas.
